p_question-3590.ja:
以下は、留学生の作文である。


私の友達

ミラー　ジョナサン

　私は近所の小さい喫茶店によく行きます。店長は林さんという70歳の男性です。林さんは私より50歳も年上ですが、私たちは友達です。

　そのお店には半年ぐらい前から行くようになりました。私が行くと、林さんはいつも[19]。それで、私たちはだんだん仲がよくなりました。

　ほかのお客さんがいないとき、私たちはよくおしゃべりをしています。林さんはコーヒーの種類について詳しくて、私はコーヒーのことがよくわかるようになりました。ときどき、林さんに日本の生活で困っていることを相談することもあります。林さんはいつもいいアドバイスをくれます。[20]私たちは二人とも映画が好きなので、映画の話もよくします。この間は、一緒に映画を見に行きました。

　日本で林さん[21]友達ができることは予想していませんでした。こんなに年上の友達ができたのは初めてでしたが、年齢の差は重要ではないことがわかりました。日本で林さんに[22]よかったです。

The following is an essay by an international student.


My Friend

Miller Jonathan

I often go to a small café in my neighborhood. The manager is a 70-year-old man named Mr. Hayashi. Mr. Hayashi is 50 years older than I am, but we are friends.

I started going to that café about half a year ago. When I go, Mr. Hayashi always [19]. Because of that, we gradually became close.

When there are no other customers, we often chat. Mr. Hayashi knows a lot about types of coffee, and I’ve come to understand coffee well. Sometimes I also consult him about difficulties I have with life in Japan. He always gives me good advice. [20] Since we both like movies, we often talk about films as well. The other day, we went to see a movie together.

I didn’t expect that I would be able to make a friend [21] Mr. Hayashi in Japan. It was the first time I’d made a friend so much older than me, but I realized that the age difference isn’t important. I’m glad I [22] Mr. Hayashi in Japan.

p_question-3594.ja:
(1) 
これは、野村さんが部長会議の出席予定者に書いたメールである。


部長会議にご出席予定の皆様

あさって12日(金)の部長会議について、ご連絡します。

　天気予報では、明日の夜、台風が東京の近くを通ることが予想されています。そのため、あさってまで交通機関に影響が残る可能性があります。明日の予報で台風が東京を通ることが確実になった場合は、来週に会議を延期することにします。最終的なご連絡は、明日11日(木)の15時にメールでいたします。

以上、よろしくお願いします。

第一営業部

野村マリア

(1) 
This is an email written by Ms. Nomura to the expected attendees of the department heads’ meeting.


To everyone planning to attend the department heads’ meeting

This is to inform you about the department heads’ meeting on Friday the 12th, the day after tomorrow.

According to the weather forecast, a typhoon is expected to pass near Tokyo tomorrow night. Therefore, there is a possibility that transportation will still be affected until the day after tomorrow. If tomorrow’s forecast confirms that the typhoon will pass through Tokyo, we will postpone the meeting to next week. The final notice will be sent by email at 3:00 p.m. tomorrow, Thursday the 11th.

Thank you for your cooperation.

First Sales Division

Maria Nomura

p_question-3595.ja:
(2)
　高校生の孫が「来週、Eスポーツ大会に出るんだ」と言った。コンピューターゲームの大会だと説明され、私は不思議な気がした。なぜ、それがスポーツの大会なのだろうか。

　聞いてみると、英語の「スポーツ」には、ルールを守りながら勝ったり負けたりすることを楽しむものという意味があるそうだ。それで、最近ではコンピューターゲームもスポーツと言うらしい。

　体を動かすことをスポーツだと思っていた私は驚いた。

(2)
My high-school-aged grandchild said, “Next week I’m going to compete in an e-sports tournament.” When it was explained to me that it was a computer game tournament, I found it strange. Why would that be a sports tournament?

When I asked, I was told that in English, “sports” can mean activities where you enjoy winning and losing while following rules. Because of that, recently people also call computer games a sport.

I was surprised, since I had thought sports meant moving your body.

p_question-3597.ja:
(4)

　コーヒーを飲むと眠れなくなるから、寝る前には飲まないという人が多いだろう。 しかし最近、昼寝の前にはコーヒーを飲むといいと言われるようになってきた。

　昼寝をすると起きたあとの集中力が上がるが、長く寝るのはよくない。そこで、飲むと目が覚めるというコーヒーの効果を利用するのだ。この効果は、飲んでから約30分後に出るので、昼寝の前に飲むと、30分ぐらいで自然に目が覚める。

　ぜひやってみてほしい。

(4)

Many people avoid drinking coffee before bed because it keeps them from sleeping. However, recently it has come to be said that it’s good to drink coffee before taking a nap.

Napping increases your concentration after you wake up, but sleeping too long isn’t good. That’s where you can make use of coffee’s effect of waking you up. This effect appears about 30 minutes after drinking, so if you drink coffee before a nap, you’ll wake up naturally in about 30 minutes.

Give it a try.

p_question-3598.ja:
(1)

　私は、前から自分で野菜を育てたいと思っていました。私のうちには庭もベランダもないので、①詰めていたのですが、先月、友達から部屋の中でホウレンソウを育てているという話を聞きました。室内でできるなら私もやってみたいと思い、準備をしました。

　外に似た環境がいいだろうと考え、窓の近くで育て始めたら、4日で芽が出始めました。少しずつ大きくなるのがうれしかったです。ところが、だんだん葉が黄色くなって、全部枯れてしまったのです。太陽の光が足りなかったのかもしれないと思いました。

　②がっかりして、友達に電話をして聞いてみると、ホウレンソウは夜になったら、光が当たってはいけないということがわかりました。私の場合は、部屋の電気の光がよくなかったようです。友達は、段ボール箱でホウレンソウにカバーをしているのだそうです。

　私も同じようにやってみたら、③今度は元気に育ちました。サラダにして食べたら、とてもおいしかったです。

(1)

I had long wanted to grow vegetables myself. Since my home has neither a garden nor a balcony, I had ①been giving up on it, but last month I heard from a friend that they were growing spinach inside their room. If it can be done indoors, I wanted to try too, so I made preparations.

Thinking an environment similar to outdoors would be best, I started growing them near the window, and in four days the sprouts began to appear. I was happy to see them grow little by little. However, the leaves gradually turned yellow and everything withered. I thought maybe there wasn’t enough sunlight.

②Feeling disappointed, I called my friend to ask, and learned that spinach must not be exposed to light once it’s nighttime. In my case, it seems the room light was the problem. My friend said they cover the spinach with a cardboard box.

When I tried doing the same, ③this time it grew vigorously. When I ate it as a salad, it was very delicious.

p_question-3601.ja:
(2)

　日本に留学したばかりのころ、文房具店に入って驚いてしまった。私の国では、シャープペンシルはあまり売られていないが、その店には100円の安い物から5000円以上する高い物まで、すごい数のシャーペンが並んでいたのだ。芯も太さが0.3mmから1.5mmまであって、濃さもいろいろな物が売られていた。

　イギリスで生まれたシャーペンだが、初めは1.5mmや1.0mmの芯しかなかった。だが、約60年前、ある日本の会社が、複雑な漢字でも美しく書けるよう、世界で初めて0.5mmの折れにくい芯を作ることにチャレンジして、成功したのだそうだ。それからシャーペンが日本中で使われるようになったらしい。

　私は、来日してからずっとシャーペンを愛用している。鉛筆のように先が丸くならず、小さい字がきれいに書けるからだ。今、使っているのは安い物だが、来年、就職したら、少しいい物を買おうと思っている。

(2)

When I had just come to study in Japan, I went into a stationery store and was really surprised. In my country, mechanical pencils aren’t sold much, but that shop had an amazing number of them, from cheap ones costing 100 yen to expensive ones over 5,000 yen. The leads ranged in thickness from 0.3 mm to 1.5 mm, and various levels of darkness were sold as well.

Mechanical pencils were born in the U.K., but at first there were only 1.5 mm and 1.0 mm leads. However, about 60 years ago, a Japanese company took on the challenge of creating, for the first time in the world, a 0.5 mm lead that wouldn’t break easily so that even complex kanji could be written beautifully—and they succeeded. After that, mechanical pencils came to be used all over Japan.

Since coming to Japan, I’ve been a devoted user of mechanical pencils. Unlike regular pencils, the tip doesn’t become rounded, and I can write small characters neatly. The one I’m using now is inexpensive, but when I get a job next year, I plan to buy a slightly nicer one.

p_question-3604.ja:
野球やサッカーなどのプロスポーツの経営では、試合会場に来てくれる客を増やすことが重要だ。また見たいと思うようないい試合が続けば、客は自然に増えるだろう。しかし、どんなに選手が努力しても、そのような試合をずっと続けるのは難しい。そこで、①経営者のさまざまな工夫が必要になる。

　その一つに、②試合会場の工夫がある。例えば、ある会場では、試合がある日に、いろいろな飲食店の食べ物や飲み物を1000種類も選べるようにしている。また、バーベキュー（注1）をしながら試合が見られる観客席を作っているところもある。会場を楽しく過ごせる場所にして、また来たいと思ってもらおうという考え方だ。経営という点では、試合を見ること以外にも会場に来る目的を作ることが重要なのだ。

　もちろん、試合を楽しむために来る客を増やす工夫もしている。例えば、新しい客に来てもらうために、無料のチケットを配るという方法がある。一度会場に来て試合を楽しんでくれたら、次はチケットを買って来てくれる可能性が広がるという考えからだ。

　ただ、③この考え方には反対意見もある。無料のチケットで来た客は、ふだんのチケットを高く感じてしまう。すると、結局チケットを買わなくなるので、収入は増えないと考える経営者もいる。

　プロスポーツの経営者は、客に来てもらうために、いろいろな工夫をしている。手段はさまざまだが、客に満足してもらい、何度も来てもらいたいという気持ちは共通しているのだ。

（注）バーベキュー：肉や野菜を焼いて食べること

In managing professional sports such as baseball and soccer, increasing the number of spectators who come to the venue is important. If good matches that people want to see continue, the number of spectators will naturally increase. However, no matter how hard the players work, it is difficult to keep such matches going all the time. Therefore, ①various innovations by the managers become necessary.

One of these is ②innovations at the venue. For example, at one venue, on game days, spectators can choose from as many as 1,000 kinds of food and drink from various eateries. There are also places that have created seats where you can watch the game while having a barbecue (note 1). The idea is to make the venue a place where people can have fun and want to come again. From a management standpoint, it is important to create purposes for coming to the venue in addition to just watching the game.

Of course, efforts are also made to increase spectators who come to enjoy the game itself. For example, to attract new spectators, there is a method of distributing free tickets. The idea is that if they come once and enjoy the game, there is a greater possibility that they will buy a ticket and come next time.

However, there are opposing opinions to ③this way of thinking. Spectators who came with free tickets may feel that regular tickets are expensive. As a result, they may end up not buying tickets, so some managers believe revenue will not increase.

Managers of professional sports make various efforts to get spectators to come. The methods vary, but their shared intention is to satisfy spectators and have them come again and again.

(Note) Barbecue: grilling meat and vegetables to eat

questions.stem: すみません、弟がこれを割ってしまいました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: わって is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"すみません、弟がこれを割ってしまいました。"  
→ "I'm sorry, my younger brother broke this."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "割って" is read as **わって**, which means *to break* or *to split*. This reading fits because the context involves something being broken.

**Other Options**  
1. けって – Means *to kick* (kanji: 蹴って), which is not related to breaking.  
2. おって – Means *to fold* (kanji: 折って), which doesn't fit the context.  
4. さわって – Means *to touch* (kanji: 触って), which is not applicable to breaking something.

Only **わって** correctly matches the kanji **割って** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「割(わ)って」 — 깨다·쪼개다. 동생이 무언가를 깨뜨린 상황.
오답: 1 けって는 「蹴(け)って」(차다), 2 おって는 「折(お)って」(접다·꺾다), 4 さわって는 「触(さわ)って」(만지다).
포인트: 割=わ(る)/カツ.

questions.stem: ピアノを基本から習いたい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: きほん is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"ピアノを基本から習いたい。"  
→ "I want to learn the piano from the basics."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "基本" is read as **きほん**, which means *basics* or *fundamentals*. This is the correct reading of the kanji in the sentence, as it refers to starting with foundational skills.

**Other Options**  
1. ぎほん – Not a standard reading of 基本.  
2. しほん – Refers to *capital* (資本), which is unrelated here.  
4. じほん – Not a standard reading of 基本.

Only **きほん** matches the kanji **基本** in this context referring to *basics*.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「基本(きほん)」 — 기본·기초. 「基本から習(なら)う」(기초부터 배우다)와 자연스럽게 연결.
오답: 1 ぎほん·2 しほん·4 じほん은 모두 없는 음.
포인트: 基=キ, 本=ホン.

questions.stem: かばんは横においてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: よこ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"かばんは横においてください。"  
→ "Please place the bag on its side."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "横" is read as **よこ**, which means *side* or *beside*. This is the appropriate reading for positioning, as specified in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
2. そば – Means *nearby* or *beside* (kanji: 側), but does not match 横.  
3. となり – Means *next to* (kanji: 隣), but does not fit the reading for 横.  
4. ゆか – Means *floor* (kanji: 床), and is unrelated to 横.

Only **よこ** is the correct reading for **横** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「横(よこ)」 — 옆·가로. 「横におく」(옆에 놓다).
오답: 2 そば는 「側(そば)」, 3 となり는 「隣(となり)」, 4 ゆか는 「床(ゆか)」로 한자가 다름.
포인트: 横=よこ(훈독)/オウ.

questions.stem: この仕事は今日が最初です。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: さいしょ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"この仕事は今日が最初です。"  
→ "Today is the first day of this job."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "最初" is read as **さいしょ**, which means *first* or *beginning*. This reading fits the context of the sentence, indicating the start of the work.

**Other Options**  
2. さいしゅう – Means *last* or *final* (kanji: 最終), not suitable for describing the beginning.  
3. さいしょう – Although it might suggest *smallest* or *minimum* (kanji: 最小), it doesn't match 最初.  
4. さいしゅ – Not a standard reading of 最初 and does not convey the intended meaning.

Only **さいしょ** appropriately corresponds to the kanji **最初** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「最初(さいしょ)」 — 처음·맨 처음.
오답: 2 さいしゅう는 「最終(さいしゅう)」(최종), 3 さいしょう는 「最小/最少」(최소), 4 さいしゅ는 없는 음.
포인트: 初=ショ/はじ(め). 「最初⇔最後」 짝으로 외우기.

questions.stem: 昨日、山田さん日本を返しました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: かえしました is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"昨日、山田さん日本を返しました。"  
→ "Yesterday, Mr. Yamada returned to Japan."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "返しました" is read as **かえしました**, which means *returned*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence, indicating someone returning to a place.

**Other Options**  
1. わかしました – Would suggest *boiled* (kanji: 沸かしました), not appropriate here.  
2. もどしました – Means *put back* or *returned something* (kanji: 戻しました), but contextually incorrect here.  
3. かしました – Means *lent* (kanji: 貸しました), and does not fit the context of returning.

Only **かえしました** matches the kanji **返しました** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「返(かえ)しました」 — 돌려주다·반납하다.
오답: 1 わかしました는 「沸(わ)かしました」(끓이다), 2 もどしました는 「戻(もど)しました」(되돌리다), 3 かしました는 「貸(か)しました」(빌려주다).
포인트: 返=かえ(す)/ヘン. 「貸す⇔返す」 짝.

questions.stem: この包丁は使いやすい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: ほうちょう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"この包丁は使いやすい。"  
→ "This kitchen knife is easy to use."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "包丁" is read as **ほうちょう**, which refers to a *kitchen knife*. This reading is commonly used in Japanese to describe knives specifically designed for kitchen use.

**Other Options**  
1. ぼうちょう – Incorrect interpretation and not a standard reading of 包丁.  
3. ほうちょ – An incomplete reading and not used in this context.  
4. ぼうちょ – Not a recognized or standard reading for the kanji 包丁.

Only **ほうちょう** correctly represents the kanji **包丁** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「包丁(ほうちょう)」 — 식칼.
오답: 1·4 ぼうちょう, 3 ほうちょ, 4 ぼうちょ는 모두 잘못된 음. 「丁」은 여기서 「チョウ」 장음.
포인트: 包=ホウ, 丁=チョウ.

questions.stem: 適当なやり方を教えてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: てきとう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"適当なやり方を教えてください。"  
→ "Please teach me the appropriate method."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "適当" is read as **てきとう**, which means *appropriate* or *suitable*. This is the correct reading and meaning in the context of the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. てきどう – Not a standard reading of 適当; not used in this context.  
3. ていとう – Can mean *affordable* or *reasonable* (kanji: 定当), but not appropriate here.  
4. ていどう – Incorrect reading; does not correspond to 適当.

Only **てきとう** matches the kanji **適当** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「適当(てきとう)」 — 적당하다·적절하다.
오답: 1 てきどう·3 ていとう·4 ていどう는 없는 음.
포인트: 適=テキ, 当=トウ. 비슷한 「適切(てきせつ)」도 함께 기억.

questions.stem: 部屋が狭いので、大きな家具は置けません。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: かぐ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"部屋が狭いので、大きな家具は置けません。"  
→ "Because the room is small, I cannot place big furniture."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "家具" is read as **かぐ**, which means *furniture*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. やぐう – Not a standard reading of 家具.  
2. かぐう – A misreading, does not exist in standard Japanese.  
3. やぐ – Does not relate to 家具; suggests something like a *camp bed* (簡易ベッド), but irrelevant here.

Only **かぐ** matches the kanji **家具** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「家具(かぐ)」 — 가구.
오답: 1 やぐう·2 かぐう·3 やぐ는 잘못된 음. 「具」는 「グ」(단음).
포인트: 家=カ/いえ, 具=グ.

questions.stem: この質問をといてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 解いて is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"この質問をといてください。"  
→ "Please solve this question."

**Explanation**  
The word "といて" comes from the verb *解く* (とく), which means *to solve* or *to unravel*. In this context, it refers to solving a question or problem.

**Other Options**  
1. 試いて（こころみいて）– This is read as "こころみいて", deriving from the verb *試みる* meaning *to try* or *to attempt*. It doesn’t convey the meaning of solving.

2. 答いて（こたえて）– The reading is "こたえて", from the verb *答える* (こたえる), meaning *to answer*. While related, it's not the correct representation for "といて".

4. 調いて（しらべいて）– This is read as "しらべいて", from the verb *調べる* (しらべる), which means *to investigate* or *to examine*. It is unrelated to the action of solving a question.

Only **解いて** correctly represents the word "といて" in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:

오답: 1 試(ため)す, 2 答(こた)える, 4 調(しら)べる는 읽는 법과 의미가 다름. 「といて」로 읽히는 동사는 「解く」뿐.
포인트: 「問題(もんだい)を解く」 = 문제를 풀다.

questions.stem: 今日は少しこしが痛い。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 腰 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"今日は少しこしが痛い。"  
→ "My lower back hurts a little today."

**Explanation**  
The word "こし" means *lower back* or *waist*, and the correct kanji representation is **腰**. It is commonly used to refer to the area around the lower back or hips.

**Other Options**  
1. 胸（むね）– The reading of this kanji is "むね", and it means *chest* or *breast*. It does not match the word "こし".

2. 腹（はら）– The reading is "はら", and it means *belly* or *stomach*. It’s unrelated to the intended meaning.

3. 脇（わき）– This is read as "わき", meaning *side* or *armpit*, which doesn’t fit the word "こし".

Only **腰** correctly represents the word "こし" in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 入学式のよくしゅうから講義が始まる。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 翌週 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"入学式のよくしゅうから講義が始まる。"  
→ "Lectures will begin the week following the entrance ceremony."

**Explanation**  
The word "よくしゅう" means *the following week*, and the correct kanji representation is **翌週**. It is used to indicate the week after the current or specified week, which fits the context of when lectures will begin.

**Other Options**  
2. 次週（じしゅう）– The reading of this kanji is "じしゅう", and it means *next week*. While similar in meaning, it typically refers to the immediate next week rather than *the week following a specific event*.

3. 明週（めいしゅう）– The reading is "めいしゅう", but this is not a standard term in Japanese. The kanji combination is not used for "よくしゅう".

4. 遠週（えんしゅう）– This is read as "えんしゅう", and it doesn’t represent a meaningful term for *weeks* in Japanese.

Only **翌週** correctly represents the word "よくしゅう" for the context provided.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「翌週(よくしゅう)」 — 다음 주.
오답: 2 次週·3 明週·4 遠週는 잘 쓰지 않는 표기. 「翌〜」 = 「翌日(よくじつ)」·「翌年(よくねん)」.
포인트: 翌=ヨク (다음의 ~).

questions.stem: 今朝は気温がひくかったので、水が凍っていた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 低かった is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"今朝は気温がひくかったので、水が凍っていた。"  
→ "This morning, the temperature was low, so the water was frozen."

**Explanation**  
The word "ひくかった" means *was low*, specifically in the context of temperature or height, and the correct kanji representation is **低かった**. This is appropriate when describing temperatures that cause freezing.

**Other Options**  
1. 寒かった（さむかった）– The reading is "さむかった", meaning *was cold*. Although a similar concept, this does not convey the idea of "low" for temperature measurement in this context.

2. 底かった（そこかった）– The reading of this kanji is "そこかった", which is incorrect and does not convey a valid meaning. The kanji 底 typically means *bottom* or *depth*.

4. 暑かった（あつかった）– This is read as "あつかった", meaning *was hot*. It is the opposite of the intended meaning and does not fit the context of freezing water.

Only **低かった** accurately represents the word "ひくかった" in the context of this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「低(ひく)かった」 — 낮았다(기온·높이).
오답: 1 寒(さむ)い는 「さむかった」, 2 底는 「そこ」, 4 暑(あつ)い는 「あつかった」로 읽음.
포인트: 「低い⇔高い」. 기온은 「低い/高い」.

questions.stem: 湖はどのほうこうにありますか。

"湖はどのほうこうにありますか。"  
→ "In which direction is the lake?"

3. 法向（ほうこう）– The reading is the same as the correct answer, "ほうこう", but these kanji mean *legal direction*, which is not applicable here.

4. 法角（ほうかく）– This is also read as "ほうかく", but these characters do not form a valid word in this context.

Only **方向** correctly represents the word "ほうこう" in this sentence, pertaining to asking for directions.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 学校のきそくがすこし変わるそうだ。

**Question Translation**  
"学校のきそくがすこし変わるそうだ。"  
→ "It seems that the school rules will change a little."

questions.stem: 野球が好きな友人の（　　　）で、私も野球に興味を持った。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 影響 (えいきょう) is correct. It means "influence" or "impact."

**Question Translation**  
Due to a baseball-loving friend's influence, I also became interested in baseball.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes how the speaker developed an interest in baseball because of their friend who likes baseball. The word "影響" (えいきょう) means "influence," and it fits perfectly here as it conveys the idea that the friend's passion for baseball affected or inspired the speaker's interest.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 指定 (してい) means "designation" or "specification." It doesn't fit in this context as it refers to something being designated or specified, which is unrelated to how interests develop.
- Answer 2: 申請 (しんせい) means "application" or "request." This term doesn't fit because the sentence is about influence, not making an application or request.
- Answer 4: 通知 (つうち) means "notification" or "notice." It is not suitable here because the context isn't about receiving a notification; it's about being influenced by someone's interests.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The concept of 影響 (えいきょう) is frequently used to describe how people, events, or circumstances can influence one's thoughts, behaviors, or interests. Recognizing how certain nouns form natural collocations in sentences is important for proper interpretation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「影響(えいきょう)」 — 영향. 「友人の影響で」(친구의 영향으로).

포인트: 「Nの影響で〜」 = N의 영향으로 ~.

questions.stem: 私は歌が下手なので、歌の上手な人が（　　　）です。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: うらやましい is correct. It means "envious" or "jealous."

**Question Translation**  
I am not good at singing, so I feel envious of people who are good at singing.

**Explanation**  
In this sentence, the speaker admits they are not good at singing and then comments on how they feel about people who are good at it. The correct word to express a feeling of wanting to have the same ability is "うらやましい," which conveys envy or jealousy towards someone who has something desirable that you lack. Therefore, "うらやましい" best fits the emotional context here.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 残念（ざんねん）means "regrettable" or "unfortunate." This doesn't fit the context because having a feeling of regret or misfortune doesn't match the comparison between abilities.
- Answer 2: なつかしい means "nostalgic" or "dear (from the past)." This is not suitable as it refers to a fond memory rather than a reaction to someone's current skill or ability.
- Answer 4: 不安（ふあん）means "anxious" or "uneasy." This term is more about worry or concern, not the feeling one experiences when they admire someone's abilities.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Recognizing adjectives that describe emotions and applying them correctly in context is crucial in Japanese. Words like "うらやましい" are i-adjectives, and understanding how they fit into sentences helps in accurately conveying personal feelings or states in various situations.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「うらやましい」 — 부럽다.
오답: 1 残念(아쉽다), 2 なつかしい(그립다), 4 不安(불안)은 노래 잘하는 사람을 보고 느끼는 감정으로 어색.
포인트: 자신과 비교해 「상대가 부럽다」 → うらやましい.

questions.stem: ゴールまでもう少しだったので、足の痛みを（　　　）して走ってきた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 我慢（がまん） is correct. It means "perseverance" or "endurance."

**Question Translation**  
Because the finish line was just a little further, I continued running while enduring the pain in my feet.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes a situation where the speaker continues to run despite feeling pain in their feet. The word 我慢 (がまん) is used to express enduring, tolerating, or putting up with discomfort or pain. It fits the context as the individual is persevering despite physical pain to reach the goal.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 苦労（くろう）means "hardship" or "trouble." It isn't typically used to describe the act of enduring pain in a context like running towards a goal.
- Answer 3: 遠慮（えんりょ）means "restraint" or "hesitation." It indicates holding back or being reserved, which doesn't apply to enduring pain.
- Answer 4: 努力（どりょく）means "effort" or "endeavor." While related to trying hard, it doesn't specifically convey enduring pain or discomfort.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The word 我慢 (がまん) is commonly associated with enduring physical or mental pain without giving up, demonstrating perseverance in challenging situations. Recognizing this term is important as it frequently appears in contexts involving personal struggles or challenges in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「我慢(がまん)」 — 참다·견디다. 「痛みを我慢する」(아픔을 참다).
오답: 2 苦労(고생), 3 遠慮(사양), 4 努力(노력)는 「아픔을 ~하다」와 어울리지 않음.
포인트: 「我慢する」 = 참다.

questions.stem: 林さんは会議ではいつも（　　　）ので、あまり自分の意見を言わない。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 消極的 (しょうきょくてき) is correct. It means "passive" or "unassertive."

**Question Translation**  
Mr. Hayashi is always (　　　) in meetings, so he hardly ever expresses his own opinions.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence involves behavior during meetings, specifically related to not expressing personal opinions. 消極的 (しょうきょくてき) means "passive" or "unassertive," which fits perfectly since a passive person is unlikely to actively share their thoughts or speak up, especially in a meeting scenario.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 人工的 (じんこうてき) means "artificial" or "man-made." It does not relate to behavior or participation in a meeting, thus it is not applicable in this context.
- Answer 3: 自動的 (じどうてき) means "automatic." This term describes actions done or processes occurring without thinking or effort, which does not correlate with the behavior of not expressing opinions in meetings.
- Answer 4: 間接的 (かんせつてき) means "indirect." While it could relate to communication style, it doesn’t directly explain why someone would not share personal opinions. It suggests a different nuance, such as communicating in a roundabout way, rather than not expressing opinions at all.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
消極的 (しょうきょくてき) is used to describe someone who is inactive, unassertive, or lacks initiative in social or work situations. It's often contrasted with 積極的 (せっきょくてき), which means "active" or "assertive," marking the importance of recognizing the differences between positive and negative expressions of participation and engagement.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「消極的(しょうきょくてき)」 — 소극적. 회의에서 의견을 잘 말하지 않음.
오답: 1 人工的(인공적), 3 自動的(자동적), 4 間接的(간접적)은 사람 성격 묘사로 부적합.
포인트: 「消極的⇔積極的(せっきょくてき)」.

questions.stem: このバスは駅を出発してから、図書館を（　　　）して美術館へ行きます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 経由 (けいゆ) is correct. It means "via" or "by way of."

**Question Translation**  
This bus, after departing from the station, goes to the art museum via the library.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes a route taken by a bus, indicating that it will stop by or pass through a specific place before reaching its final destination. The term 経由 (けいゆ) is used to describe the path or places a mode of transportation passes through before arriving at the endpoint, making it the perfect fit for this context as the bus is going to the art museum via the library.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 移動 (いどう) means "movement" or "transfer." It does not fit the context of going through or stopping at a place. It focuses more on the action of moving rather than the route.
- Answer 3: 進行 (しんこう) means "progression" or "advance." It implies moving forward or making progress but doesn’t specify the route or intermediary stops.
- Answer 4: 下車 (げしゃ) means "getting off (a vehicle)." It doesn’t fit the context of describing an intermediary stop or pathway in a travel route.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
経由 is commonly used in transport-related contexts to indicate the places or routes that are part of a journey. Understanding this term is important when discussing travel routes, itineraries, or logistical paths in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「経由(けいゆ)」 — 경유. 「Aを経由してB」(A를 거쳐 B로).
오답: 1 移動(이동), 3 進行(진행), 4 下車(하차)는 「~를 거쳐서」 의미가 안 됨.
포인트: 「〜を経由して」 = ~를 경유해서.

questions.stem: あの兄弟はとても（　　　）ので、ほとんどけんかをしないそうです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 仲 (なか) is correct. It means "relationship" or "companionship," especially denoting a positive relationship.

**Question Translation**  
It is said that those brothers get along very well, so they almost never quarrel.

**Explanation**  
In the context of the sentence, the word needed is one that describes the quality of the relationship between the brothers. 仲が良い (なかがよい) is a common expression used to describe people who have a good, friendly relationship, which aligns perfectly with the context of the brothers who do not often fight because of their good relationship.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 組 (くみ) means "set" or "group." It doesn't fit in this context as it refers to an organizational grouping and does not describe the nature of a relationship.
- Answer 2: 間 (あいだ) means "between" or "among." While it can describe space or time between entities, it fails to convey the qualitative aspect of the relationship needed here.
- Answer 3: 形 (かたち) means "shape" or "form." This option doesn’t fit as it refers to physical form or appearance, which is not relevant to the quality of a relationship.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
仲が良い (なかがよい) is an idiomatic expression widely used to denote a good, harmonious relationship between people. Understanding such common expressions is essential for grasping nuanced implications in everyday conversations and written texts.
questions.expl_ko:

포인트: 「仲がいい/悪い」 짝으로 외우기.

questions.stem: 家のドアを開けるために、ロボットの中から鍵を（　　　）。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**

**Question Translation**  
In order to open the house door, (I) took out the key from inside the robot.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence implies an action of retrieving something from inside another object (the robot), specifically, the key. 取り出した fits this context because it is a commonly used verb meaning "to take out" or "to extract," which makes sense when retrieving a key.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 取り付けた (とりつけた) means "attached" or "installed." This doesn’t fit the context as the sentence describes taking a key out, not attaching it.

Recognizing compound verbs like 取り出す (to take out) is essential in understanding actions taken in context. The prefix 取り (とり) indicates grasping or holding, combined with 出す (だす), meaning to take out, clearly indicating the action of extracting something.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: たくさんのことを（　　　）言われても、覚えられません。

**Question Translation**  
Even if I am told many things at once, I cannot remember them.

The sentence addresses the difficulty of remembering when presented with "many things" simultaneously. The phrase 一度に (いちどに) fits the context as it conveys the sense of receiving information all at once, which can be overwhelming, thus explaining why the speaker cannot remember.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 偶然(ぐうぜん) means "by chance" or "coincidentally." It doesn't match the intended meaning, as the sentence is about simultaneously receiving information, not the element of chance.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "一度に" is useful for expressing actions or events happening simultaneously. Recognizing temporal adverbs like this is important for understanding the sequence and timing of actions in Japanese, which can significantly affect sentence meaning.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: このホテルの空港からの（　　　）がいいので、外国人からのお客が多い。

questions.stem: 大好きな歌手のコンサートに行きたかったが、チケットが高くて買えないので行くのを（　　　）。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: あきらめた is correct. It means "gave up."

**Question Translation**  
I wanted to go to a concert of my favorite singer, but the tickets are too expensive and I can't buy them, so I (　　　) going.

**Explanation**  
The context of this sentence involves wanting to do something but being unable to due to circumstances—in this case, the high price of tickets. The verb あきらめた (諦めた) means "to give up" or "to abandon," which aptly describes the action of deciding not to pursue something because it is unattainable or impractical. Therefore, it best fits the space meant to express that the speaker gave up on the idea of going to the concert.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: きらった (嫌った) means "hated" or "disliked." It doesn't fit the context as the speaker wanted to go to the concert, showing interest rather than dislike.
- Answer 2: うたがった (疑った) means "doubted" or "suspected." This term doesn't work because the sentence is about giving up on going to the concert, not doubting anything about it.
- Answer 4: うばった (奪った) means "took away" or "snatched." This doesn't align with the context of making a personal decision to not go; it indicates an action of taking something away from someone else.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb あきらめる (諦める) is often used in contexts where a person has made a conscious decision to stop pursuing a goal or desire due to external challenges or limitations. Recognizing when a situation is described as unattainable is key to identifying this expression correctly.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「あきらめた」 — 포기했다. 표가 비싸서 가는 것을 포기.
오답: 1 きらった(싫어했다), 2 うたがった(의심했다), 4 うばった(빼앗았다)는 문맥에 안 맞음.
포인트: 「〜のをあきらめる」 = ~하는 것을 포기하다.

questions.stem: タクシーの乗り場がわからなくて、駅の周りを（　　　）歩き回った。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: うろうろ is correct. It means "wandering aimlessly" or "loitering."

**Question Translation**  
I didn't know where the taxi stand was, so I wandered aimlessly around the station.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence involves someone who cannot find the taxi stand and is thus moving without a clear direction around the station area. The word "うろうろ" fits perfectly here as it conveys the idea of moving in a way that is directionless or somewhat lost, which aligns with the scenario described.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: ときどき means "sometimes." This adverb of frequency does not describe the manner of someone's movement, so it does not fit in this context.
- Answer 3: ざあざあ describes the sound of heavy rain. It's an onomatopoeia that does not relate to how someone is walking or moving around, making it unsuitable here.
- Answer 4: ちかちか refers to a flickering or blinking effect, like lights. This term does not describe movement or behavior in a physical sense and is irrelevant to the context of walking around a station.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Understanding onomatopoeic and mimetic words is crucial in Japanese as they often capture nuances of actions and sounds vividly. The term "うろうろ" specifically describes moving with a lack of purpose, which is particularly useful in situations of uncertainty or confusion.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「うろうろ」 — 어슬렁어슬렁·갈팡질팡 헤매는 모습.
오답: 2 ときどき(때때로), 3 ざあざあ(쏴쏴, 비 소리), 4 ちかちか(반짝반짝)는 의태어 의미가 다름.
포인트: 길을 모를 때 「うろうろ歩く」.

questions.stem: この商品は売り切れました。
opts: あまり売れませんでした
全然売れませんでした
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 全然売れました is correct. It means "completely sold out."

**Question Translation**  
The sentence translates to "This product is sold out."

**Explanation**  
The phrase 売り切れました (うりきれました) means "sold out" or "completely sold," indicating that the product is no longer available for purchase because all units have been sold. The correct paraphrase should convey the idea that the product has been entirely sold. Though 全然 (ぜんぜん) usually pairs with negative expressions, in casual spoken Japanese, it can sometimes be used with positive verbs to emphasize completeness.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: ほとんど売れました means "almost sold" or "sold almost all." While it suggests that a large quantity was sold, it does not mean completely sold out, so it doesn’t capture the meaning of 売り切れました.
- Answer 3: あまり売れませんでした means "didn't sell much." This is the opposite of sold out, indicating poor sales, so it doesn’t fit the context.
- Answer 4: 全然売れませんでした is "didn't sell at all." Despite using 全然, which is part of the correct answer, the negative verb form contradicts the complete sale suggested by 売り切れました.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb 売り切れる (うりきれる) specifically means "to be sold out" and implies everything has been sold, leaving no stock remaining. It's important to distinguish between this and phrases that indicate only partial sales. Additionally, though 全然 typically accompanies negative constructions, its occasional use with positive verbs in informal settings emphasizes the completeness or totality of an action.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「ほとんど売(う)れました」 — 거의 다 팔렸다.
근거: 「売(う)り切(き)れる」 = 다 팔리다(매진). 「全然〜ました」는 부정문 외 어법이 어색하며, 매진의 의미를 살리지 못함.
오답: 2·3·4는 부정문이거나 의미가 반대.
포인트: 「売り切れ」 = 매진.

questions.stem: あそこは道がカーブしていますので、気をつけてください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 曲がって is correct. It means "to be curved" or "to turn."

**Question Translation**  
Please be careful there as the road is curved.

**Explanation**  
The sentence cautions the listener to be mindful because the road has a curve. The verb 曲がる (まがる) means "to curve" or "to bend." In this context, 曲がって (the te-form of 曲がる) accurately captures the action or state of the road being curved, making it the best fit.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 狭くなって (せまくなって) means "becoming narrow." This suggests a change in width, not direction, so it doesn't align with the notion of a curved roadway.
- Answer 3: 込んで (こんで) means "crowded." This describes a state of congestion, not the physical shape or direction of the road.
- Answer 4: 暗くなった (くらくなった) means "became dark." It refers to a change in lighting, which doesn’t fit the context of describing the physical curvature of a road.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb 曲がる is often used to describe the physical state of things that bend or curve, such as roads, paths, or even body movements. Recognizing this verb and its forms is crucial for understanding descriptions of movement and direction in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「曲(ま)がって」 — 굽어 있다.
근거: 「カーブする」 = 길이 휘어지다 → 「曲がる」와 동의.
오답: 1 狭(せま)い(좁다), 3 込(こ)む(붐비다), 4 暗(くら)い(어둡다)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: 「道がカーブしている」 = 「道が曲がっている」.

questions.stem: 今夜は家に誰もいないから退屈だ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: つまらない is correct. It means "boring" or "dull."

**Question Translation**  
Tonight, because there is no one at home, it is boring.

**Explanation**  
The sentence expresses a feeling about being alone at home. The key word 退屈 (たいくつ) means "bored" or "boredom," which describes a lack of excitement or activity. The word つまらない is a suitable paraphrase as it also means "boring" or "uninteresting," making it the best fit for expressing the speaker's sense of boredom.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 怖い (こわい) means "scary" or "frightening." It doesn’t relate to the feeling of boredom and instead conveys fear, which isn’t indicated by the context of the sentence.
- Answer 3: さびしい (寂しい) means "lonely" or "lonesome." While being alone can lead to loneliness, the original sentence focuses on the dullness from being alone, not the emotional state of loneliness.
- Answer 4: 忙しい (いそがしい) means "busy." It clearly contrasts with the context of having a lack of activity or excitement, making it an incorrect choice.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The adjective 退屈 (たいくつ) is often used to express a state of boredom due to a lack of engaging activities or company, while つまらない can describe something uninteresting or dull. Recognizing these subtle differences is crucial for accurate interpretation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「つまらない」 — 지루하다.
근거: 「退屈(たいくつ)」 = 따분함·지루함. 둘 다 재미가 없다는 뜻.
오답: 2 怖(こわ)い(무섭다), 3 さびしい(쓸쓸하다), 4 忙(いそが)しい(바쁘다)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: 退屈 = つまらない.

questions.stem: テレビで紹介された店にさっそく行った。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: すぐに is correct. It means "immediately" or "right away."

**Question Translation**  
I went to the store that was introduced on TV immediately.

**Explanation**  
The sentence context indicates that there is an immediate action taken after seeing something on TV. The word さっそく (早速) is commonly used to express doing something promptly or without delay. The best paraphrase for this is すぐに, which conveys the sense of urgency or immediacy matching さっそく.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 初めて (はじめて) means "for the first time." This implies that the action is the first occurrence, not necessarily immediate, which doesn't capture the meaning of さっそく.
- Answer 2: やっと means "finally" or "at last." This implies a delay before the action, opposite to the immediate action suggested by さっそく.
- Answer 3: たまに means "occasionally." It indicates infrequent action, which does not match the urgency suggested by さっそく.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The expression さっそく (早速) is frequently used in both formal and informal settings to emphasize the promptness of an action after a trigger. Understanding such adverbs and their specific nuances is crucial as they define the immediacy of the action in context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「すぐに」 — 즉시·바로.
근거: 「さっそく(早速)」 = 곧바로·즉시.
오답: 1 初(はじ)めて(처음으로), 2 やっと(겨우), 3 たまに(가끔)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: 「さっそく〜する」 = 즉시 ~하다.

questions.stem: このズボンはゆるいので、別のズボンが欲しい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 大きい is correct. It means "big" or "large."

**Question Translation**  
These pants are loose, so I want another pair.

**Explanation**  
The sentence indicates that the pants currently being worn are too loose. In Japanese, ゆるい (yurui) often implies that something is loose or not tight enough, which suggests that the pants are larger than desired. The adjective 大きい (おおきい) meaning "big" best fits this description because large pants would be loose on the wearer.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 古い (ふるい) means "old." This doesn't relate to the looseness of the pants. Rather, it describes the condition or age, not the fit.
- Answer 2: 小さい (ちいさい) means "small." This is the opposite of the intended meaning; small pants would be tight rather than loose.
- Answer 4: 薄い (うすい) means "thin." This describes the material's thickness and not the size or fit. It is irrelevant in describing why the pants are loose.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The adjective ゆるい can describe things like loose clothing or relaxed rules, emphasizing a lack of tightness or strictness. Recognizing context helps to match adjectives to the conditions being described accurately, as seen here with ゆるい meaning the pants are too large.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「大(おお)きい」 — 크다.
근거: 「ゆるい」 = 헐렁하다 → 옷·바지가 너무 큼.
오답: 1 古(ふる)い(낡다), 2 小(ちい)さい(작다), 4 薄(うす)い(얇다)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: 옷이 ゆるい → 大きい(크다).

questions.stem: 知識
opts: いつ財布を落としたか知識
はさみどこにあるか知識
パーティーには私の知識
その仕事は医学の知識
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
知識（ちしき） — knowledge, information

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
That job cannot be done without medical knowledge.

**Explanation**  
The word **知識** refers to information or understanding about a specific subject. In this context, "医学の知識" (medical knowledge) emphasizes the necessity of having specific knowledge to perform the job, making this usage appropriate and natural.

**Other Options**

① いつ財布を落としたか__知識__がありません。  
→ I have no knowledge of when I dropped my wallet.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While "知識" can mean knowledge, it is awkward in this context. The more appropriate word would be "覚え" (memory) as in "覚えがない".

② はさみどこにあるか__知識__がありますか。  
→ Do you have knowledge of where the scissors are?  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"知識" is not a suitable choice for asking about the location of objects. The word "情報" (information) or simply "知っている" (know) would be more appropriate.

③ パーティーには私の__知識__のある人も大勢来ていた。  
→ Many people at the party had my knowledge.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
This sentence implies people possessing the same set of knowledge, which sounds vague or incorrect. It would make more sense with "知り合い" (acquaintance) to denote familiarity with people.

**Usage Note**  
"知識" tends to be paired with specific fields, concepts, or skills, like "医学の知識" (medical knowledge) or "歴史の知識" (historical knowledge). It isn't used for locations, personal memories, or to describe interpersonal relationships.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「その仕事は医学の知識(ちしき)がないとできない」 — 그 일은 의학 지식이 없으면 할 수 없다.
근거: 「知識」 = 어떤 분야에 대한 정보·앎. 「医学の知識」(의학 지식)처럼 분야와 결합.
오답: 1·2는 「いつ/どこ」 의문문에 「知識」가 부자연(보통 「知らない」), 3은 사람 모임에 「知識」을 쓸 수 없음.
포인트: 「Nの知識がある/ない」.

questions.stem: ひびく
opts: 台所からおいしそうなにおいがひびいている
この技術は100年くらい前に外国からひびいた
この広場で歌を歌うと声がよくひびく
社長が代わるといううわさが会社中でひびい
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
響く（ひびく） — to resonate, to echo, to have an effect

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
When I sing in this square, my voice resonates well.

**Explanation**  
The verb **響く** (ひびく) is commonly used when describing how sound travels or resonates in a space. In this context, "声がよく響く" (the voice resonates well) is a natural and common expression, used when discussing acoustics or sound quality in an area.

**Other Options**

① 台所からおいしそうなにおいが響いている。  
→ A delicious smell is resonating from the kitchen.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"響く" is inappropriate for describing how smells travel. Instead, "漂っている" (かおっている), meaning "wafting," is suitable.

② この技術は100年くらい前に外国から響いたものだ。  
→ This technology resonated from a foreign country about a hundred years ago.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"響く" does not convey the spread or origin of ideas or technologies. A better word would be "伝わった" (was transmitted or spread).

④ 社長が代わるといううわさが会社中で響いている。  
→ The rumor that the company president will change is resonating throughout the company.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
Though grammatically possible, "響いている" is rarely used for rumors. More appropriate is "広まっている" (is spreading) or "知れ渡っている" (is widely known).

**Usage Note**  
"響く" is best suited for discussing sound and its ability to echo or resonate in a space. It is not used for describing smells, the spread of information, or technology's origins and influence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「この広場で歌を歌うと声がよく響(ひび)く」 — 이 광장에서 노래하면 소리가 잘 울린다.
근거: 「響く」 = 소리가 울리다·반향하다.
오답: 1은 냄새(におい)에 響く 사용 불가(→ 漂う), 2는 기술 전래(→ 伝わる), 4는 소문 퍼짐(→ 広がる).
포인트: 소리·음향에 「響く」.

questions.stem: 完成
opts: みんなの意見が一つに完成
娘は子どものころからの夢が完成
今建てている家が完成
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
完成（かんせい） — completion, finish

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
When the house we are building now is completed, please do come and visit us.

**Explanation**  
The word **完成** is used to indicate the completion or finishing of tangible objects or projects, such as buildings, works of art, or other substantial undertakings. "家が完成した" (the house is completed) is a perfectly natural expression, as it involves a physical structure being finished.

**Other Options**

① みんなの意見が一つに完成したので、これから発表します。  
→ Everyone’s opinions have completed into one, so we will now present.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
The idea of opinions "completing" into one is awkward. A more suitable expression is "まとまった" (come together).

② 娘は子どものころからの夢が完成して医者になった。  
→ My daughter completed her childhood dream and became a doctor.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
While "完成" sounds fitting, it isn't used for fulfilling personal dreams. Instead, use "叶った" (came true).

③ 親戚の結婚が完成したので、お祝いを贈ろうと思います。  
→ My relative's marriage has completed, so I am thinking of sending a gift.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"完成" doesn’t apply to marriage ceremonies; "済んだ" (finished) or "挙げた" (held) is preferable.

**Usage Note**  
"完成" is typically associated with the physical completion of projects or objects. It’s not used for abstract concepts like opinions, dreams, or social events like weddings.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「今建てている家が完成(かんせい)したら」 — 지금 짓고 있는 집이 완성되면.
근거: 「完成」 = (건축·작품 등 구체물의) 완성.
오답: 1 의견 일치(→ まとまる), 2 꿈 이루다(→ かなう), 3 결혼 성사(→ 決まる)에는 「完成」을 쓰지 않음.
포인트: 건물·작품에 「完成する」.

questions.stem: あわてる
opts: 昨日大雨が降ったので、今日は川の水があわてて
マラソン大会で優勝するため、私はゴールまであわてて
あのレストランは人気があるので、お店の人はいつもあわてて
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
あわてる — to panic, to rush, to be flustered

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
I overslept, so I rushed out of the house in a panic and forgot my cell phone.

**Explanation**  
The word **あわてる** describes a state of being hurried or flustered, usually due to a pressing situation. In the context of oversleeping and leaving the house in a hurry, "あわてて家を出た" (rushed out of the house in a panic) is a typical and natural usage.

**Other Options**

① 昨日大雨が降ったので、今日は川の水が__あわてて__流れている。  
→ Because it rained heavily yesterday, today the river water is flowing in a panic.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"川の水があわてて流れている" is awkward because natural elements like water don’t experience emotions or panic. A better expression would be "勢いよく流れている" (flowing forcefully).

③ マラソン大会で優勝するため、私はゴールまで__あわてて__走り続けた。  
→ To win the marathon, I continued running to the goal in a panic.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While the sentence is grammatically correct, "あわてて" implies disorganization or haste due to panic, which is not fitting for a planned and focused effort like winning a marathon. "必死に" or "一生懸命" (with all my might) would be more appropriate.

④ あのレストランは人気があるので、お店の人はいつも__あわてて__働いている。  
→ Because that restaurant is popular, the staff is always working in a panic.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
Even though "あわてて" could apply here, it implies a sense of chaos or lack of control. A more suitable phrase might be "忙しく" (busily) or "慌ただしく" (hurriedly).

**Usage Note**  
"あわてる" generally implies a sudden or urgent response to an unexpected situation and is often used for personal actions or feelings of haste. It does not naturally describe scenarios lacking a sense of individual panic, like natural phenomena or deliberate, focused efforts.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「寝坊(ねぼう)をして、あわてて家を出た」 — 늦잠을 자서 허둥지둥 집을 나왔다.
근거: 「あわてる」 = 당황하다·서두르다.
오답: 1 강물 불어남(→ 増える), 3 마라톤 결승(→ 急ぐ), 4 가게 점원(→ 忙しい)은 부자연.
포인트: 갑작스러운 상황에 당황 = あわてる.

questions.stem: 実物
opts: 服はインターネットの写真だけではなく、実物
そのときは彼女の実物
デパートで買い物をしたとき、実物
緊張したので発表のときは失敗しましたが、実物
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
実物（じつぶつ） — the real thing, the actual object

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
Rather than just the internet photos, I want to see the actual item before buying clothes.

**Explanation**  
The word **実物** refers to the tangible, physical item itself, as opposed to an image, description, or concept. In shopping or evaluating products, seeing the "actual item" is a common and practical concern.

**Other Options**

② そのときは彼女の__実物__の気持ちが分からなかった。  
→ At that time, I didn't understand her actual feelings.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"実物" is not applicable to abstract concepts such as feelings. "本当の気持ち" or "実際の気持ち" would be appropriate.

③ デパートで買い物をしたとき、__実物__が足りなかったので、クレジットカードで払った。  
→ When I shopped at the department store, I didn't have enough real things, so I paid by credit card.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
This sentence seems to confuse "お金" (money) with "実物." The term should be "現金" (cash) instead of "実物."

④ 緊張したので発表のときは失敗しましたが、__実物__はもっと上手にできました。  
→ I got nervous and failed during the presentation, but the real thing went much better.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While the sentence could be implying a practice versus actual scenario, "実物" here does not clearly convey this meaning. A better fit might be "本番" (the real event) or "本当" (truly).

**Usage Note**  
"実物" is best used when distinguishing between a physical item and its representation or expectation. It is not suited for abstract ideas, feelings, or personal capabilities.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「服はインターネットの写真だけではなく、実物(じつぶつ)を見てから買いたい」 — 옷은 인터넷 사진뿐 아니라 실물을 보고 사고 싶다.
근거: 「実物」 = 실제 물건(사진·복제와 대조).
오답: 2·3·4는 사람·경험·발표 등 추상 상황으로 「実物」 부적합.
포인트: 사진 vs 실물.

questions.stem: 久しぶりに大学時代の友人と会って、楽しい時間（　　　）過ごした。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: を is correct. It means "to" or "with," and it is used to mark the direct object of the verb in a sentence.

**Question Translation**  
I met with a college friend for the first time in a while and had a fun time.

**Explanation**  
In this sentence, the verb "過ごした" (すごした, meaning "spent" or "passed") requires a direct object marked by "を." The phrase "楽しい時間を過ごした" (たのしいじかんをすごした) means "spent a fun time," where "楽しい時間" (fun time) is the object of the verb "過ごした." Hence, "を" is the correct particle to use here.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: で is typically used to indicate a location or means, such as "at" or "by." It does not suit this sentence, as we are not specifying a place or method of spending time.
- Answer 2: に is used to indicate a direction, time, or purpose. It doesn’t pair with "過ごす" in this context since we are not talking about a time or location that aligns with "過ごした."
- Answer 3: が is used to mark the subject of a sentence, usually preceding a descriptive or intransitive verb. The topic of discussion, "楽しい時間," is the object here, not the subject, so が is not appropriate in this construction.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb "過ごす" (すごす) means "to spend (time), pass (time)." It frequently uses "を" to connect to its object, which is the time or period being spent in the activity. Recognizing common verb-object pairings and their corresponding particles is crucial for understanding how actions apply to objects in Japanese sentences.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「を」 — 직접 목적어 조사.
오답: 「時間を過(す)ごす」(시간을 보내다)가 고정 표현. 「で·に·が」로는 「過ごす」의 목적어를 표시 못함.
포인트: 「〜時間を過ごす」 = ~한 시간을 보내다.

questions.stem: 私の家の近くにはスーパーが２軒あって、（　　　）歩いて行けるので便利だ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: どちらへも is correct. It means "to both" or "to either."

**Question Translation**  
There are two supermarkets near my house, and it's convenient because I can walk to either.

**Explanation**  
The sentence discusses convenience regarding access to two supermarkets. "どちらへも" means "to either," implying that no matter which supermarket is chosen, it is possible to reach it by walking. This choice fits perfectly because the emphasis here is on the capability to walk to both locations, highlighting convenience.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: どちらから means "from either." This doesn’t fit the context because the sentence is about going to the supermarkets, not coming from them.  
- Answer 3: どちらかでも suggests "even one of them/either would suffice," which doesn’t correctly indicate the direction of moving towards each supermarket, missing the geographical movement needed in the sentence.  
- Answer 4: どちらかまで translates as "up to either," which emphasizes reaching an endpoint. However, in the context of this sentence, the focus is on the capability of reaching either location rather than emphasizing the endpoint specifically.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
A key aspect of this question is understanding the use of "どちらへも," which indicates direction or endpoint and in this context conveys the idea of reaching both destinations. The particle "へ" specifically highlights the direction toward a location, while "も" adds the meaning of inclusion, thereby expressing "to both locations." Recognizing these components is essential in navigating similar questions that involve directions or destinations in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「どちらへも」 — 양쪽 다·어느 쪽에도.
오답: 1 どちらから(어느 쪽에서부터)는 출발점, 3 どちらかでも(어느 한 쪽이라도), 4 どちらかまで(어느 한 쪽까지)는 「2곳 모두 걸어갈 수 있다」와 불일치.
포인트: 「N(장소)へも」 = ~에도. 둘 다 가능 → 「どちらへも」.

questions.stem: 子供のころからバスが大好きだったはバスの運転手（　　　）働いている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: として is correct. It means "as" or "in the capacity of."

**Question Translation**  
Since childhood, I loved buses, and now I am working as a bus driver.

**Explanation**  
The sentence is about someone who loved buses from a young age and now works as a bus driver. The term として indicates the capacity or role someone holds. Using として after "bus driver" emphasizes that the person is working in the role of a bus driver, which accurately reflects the intended meaning of the sentence.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: のほうが means "more than" or "preferably." It is used for comparisons and doesn’t logically fit the context of describing someone’s occupation or role.
- Answer 3: にとって means "for" or "to," often used to express perspective or opinion, like "for me" or "to him." It’s not suitable for describing a role or job capacity.
- Answer 4: のほかに means "in addition to" or "besides." This expression is used to add information rather than to state a role or capacity, making it unsuitable for the sentence.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The use of として (functioning as "as" or "in the capacity of") is crucial when describing roles, capacities, or positions. In this context, it correctly links the love for buses with the current role as a bus driver, highlighting the person’s occupation. Understanding such expressions helps in distinguishing roles and capacities in various contexts.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「として」 — ~로서(자격·역할).
오답: 2 のほうが(쪽이), 3 にとって(~에게 있어), 4 のほかに(~외에)는 직업·역할 표현이 아님.
포인트: 「Nとして働く/暮らす」 = N으로서 일하다/살다.

questions.stem: 今朝、寝坊して会議に遅れそうだったので、タクシーに乗ったが、道が込んでいて（　　　）遅刻してしまった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 結局 (けっきょく) is correct. It means "eventually" or "in the end."

**Question Translation**  
This morning, I overslept and was about to be late for a meeting, so I took a taxi, but the road was crowded, and I ended up being late anyway.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes a situation where the speaker took a taxi to avoid being late for a meeting but still ended up being late due to traffic. The word 結局 fits best in this context because it conveys the sense of "in the end," highlighting that despite efforts to be on time, the final result was still lateness.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- **Answer 1: つい** means "just now" or "accidentally." It suggests a casual or unintended action, not fitting in the context where the sequence of events leads to an inevitable outcome (lateness).
- **Answer 3: 次第に** means "gradually." This does not fit the context because it indicates a slow change over time, unsuitable when talking about the ultimate result of being late despite actions taken.
- **Answer 4: ほとんど** means "almost" or "nearly." It suggests that something did not happen completely, which does not align with the fact that the speaker actually ended up being late.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The use of 結局 is critical in Japanese to express an ultimate outcome after a series of events or actions. Recognizing this term helps convey outcomes or conclusions of situations regardless of intervening actions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「結局(けっきょく)」 — 결국.
오답: 1 つい(무심코), 3 次第(しだい)に(점차), 4 ほとんど(거의)는 「택시 탔지만 결과적으로 늦었다」와 안 맞음.
포인트: 노력했으나 결과적으로 ~ → 「結局」.

questions.stem: この歌を（　　　）たびに大学時代のことを思い出す。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 聞く is correct. It means "to listen" or "to hear."

**Question Translation**  
Every time I hear this song, I remember my university days.

**Explanation**  
The phrase "たびに" (度に) is used to express "every time" or "whenever" something happens. To construct this expression in Japanese, you use the dictionary form of a verb before "たびに." In this sentence, the verb "聞く" is appropriate because it’s in its dictionary form, correctly structuring the phrase as "聞くたびに."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 聞き is the -masu stem of the verb 聞く (to listen/hear), used for combining verbs in specific tenses and as a base for polite forms. It cannot be used with "たびに."
- Answer 3: 聞いた is the past tense form meaning "heard." It does not fit the "every time I [verb]" structure needed with "たびに," which requires the dictionary form.
- Answer 4: 聞いている is the "te-iru" form, meaning "listening" or "is listening/hearing." This continuous form is incorrect to pair with "たびに," which indicates a repeated or habitual action.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
When using "たびに" to express "every time" something happens, ensure the verb preceding it is in its dictionary form (neutral form, present simple). This is crucial knowledge for accurately expressing repeated or habitual actions in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「聞(き)く」 — 동사 사전형.
오답: 「Vる + たびに」(~할 때마다)가 정형. 1 ます어간, 3 た형, 4 て형은 「たびに」와 결합 불가.
포인트: 「Vる + たびに」 = ~할 때마다.

questions.stem: （大学で）Yuuki「アンさんは、卒業後はどうするんですか。」アン「国に帰りますが、帰ってからどう（　　　）まだ決めていません。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: するかは is correct. It means "whether or not (I will) do (something)."

**Question Translation**  
Yuuki: "Ann, what will you do after graduation?"  
Ann: "I will return to my country, but I haven’t decided yet what to do after returning."

**Explanation**  
In the sentence, Ann discusses a decision she hasn't made yet regarding what to do after returning to her country. The phrase "どうするかは" is suitable here because "どうするか" translates to "what to do" or "whether or not to do something," and "は" marks the topic of discussion, putting emphasis on the uncertainty of the decision.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: するかが – The particle "が" is typically used to mark the subject of a sentence, not appropriate for expressing uncertainty about a decision in this context.
- Answer 3: することは – While "すること" means "doing" or "the act of doing," this expression is more about stating a fact or discussing an action rather than the uncertainty or potential outcomes.
- Answer 4: することが – Similar to することは, this construction suggests a subject-action relationship rather than decision-making uncertainty, making it unsuitable here.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The structure "どうするか" uses the embedded question form where "か" indicates uncertainty or questioning within a sentence, commonly followed by "は" or "が" to function as the topic or subject, respectively. In this instance, the use of "は" suggests more emphasis on the uncertainty or indecision. Recognizing these structures helps with understanding nuanced expressions of contemplation or indecision in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「するかは」 — ~할지는.
오답: 2 するかが는 「決めていません」과 호응이 어색, 3·4 することは/が는 의문이 아닌 명사화로 「まだ決めていない」의 대상이 안 됨.
포인트: 의문문 명사화 「〜かは/が」 + 「決める/わかる」.

questions.stem: インターネットで買ったセーターが届いた。サイズが少し大きいかもしれないと心配していたが、実際に（　　　）ぴったりだった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 着てみると is correct. It means "when I tried it on."

**Question Translation**  
The sweater I bought on the Internet arrived. I was worried it might be a little too big, but when I tried it on, it fit perfectly.

**Explanation**  
The sentence indicates a situation in which there is concern about the size of a sweater that was purchased online. The expression 着てみると (きてみると) means "when I tried it on," which is appropriate here because it describes the action of trying on the sweater to see how it fits. This matches the context of the sentence, which describes the process of confirming the fit of the garment.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 着たのだから (きたのだから) means "because I wore it." This implies justification after wearing, but in this context, we need an expression to describe the action leading to the discovery of the fit, not a reason for wearing it.
- Answer 3: 着るとしたら (きるとしたら) means "if I were to wear it." This suggests a hypothetical situation, which does not suit the context, since the speaker already tried on the sweater.
- Answer 4: 着ていることで (きていることで) means "by wearing it." This implies a continuous action rather than a single event of trying it on, making it unsuitable for the context where the focus is on the moment of trying.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "着てみる" is an example of using the て-form of a verb followed by みる, which indicates attempting or trying something to see the outcome. It's a common construction used to express the trial of an action to observe the result, often used in contexts like trying on clothes. Recognizing when to use this form is crucial for conveying the nuance of attempting actions to gauge results in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「着(き)てみると」 — 입어 보니.
오답: 2 着たのだから(입었으니까, 이유), 3 着るとしたら(입는다면, 가정), 4 着ていることで(입고 있는 것으로)는 「실제로 입어 보니 딱 맞았다」와 안 맞음.
포인트: 「〜てみると」 = 시도해 본 결과의 발견.

questions.stem: （大学で）A「今日、授業の後、晩ご飯食べに行かない？」B「ごめん、授業の後は図書館へ行くつもりなんだ。あしたレポートを（　　　）まだ終わってなくて。」
opts: 出したほうがよくても
出さなくてもいいのに
出してはいけなくても
出さないといけないのに
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 出さないといけないのに is correct. It means "even though I have to submit it."

**Question Translation**  
Student A: "Do you want to go out for dinner after class today?"  
Student B: "Sorry, I'm planning to go to the library after class. I still haven’t finished my report even though I have to submit it tomorrow."

**Explanation**  
The sentence context is about having an obligation or necessity to submit a report that isn't finished yet. "出さないといけない" (dasu nai to ikenai) is a phrase indicating necessity or obligation, meaning "have to submit." The ending "のに" (noni) adds a contrastive nuance similar to "even though" or "but," which indicates that despite the obligation, the task isn’t completed.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 出したほうがよくても (dashita hou ga yokute mo) means "even though it would be better to submit," implying a suggestion rather than a necessity. It doesn't fit the context, which involves a requirement or obligation.
- Answer 2: 出さなくてもいいのに (dasanakute mo ii no ni) means "even though I don't have to submit." This contradicts the required obligation to submit the report by tomorrow.
- Answer 3: 出してはいけなくても (dashite wa ikenakute mo) means "even though I must not submit it," which does not fit the context as there's a clear obligation to submit the report.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
出さないといけない is a common expression indicating necessity or requirement. It combines 出す (dasu, to submit) with ないといけない (nai to ikenai), which is used to express obligation or something that must be done. The use of "のに" (noni) highlights contrast, showing discrepancy between the obligation and the current situation. Recognizing these patterns helps in understanding sentences with contrasting elements or obligations.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「出(だ)さないといけないのに」 — 내야 하는데도.
오답: 1·2·3은 「~해도 좋다/~안 해도 된다/~해서는 안 된다」로 「아직 못 끝냈다」 상황과 맞지 않음. 4의 「のに」가 안타까움을 표현.
포인트: 「Vないといけない」(해야 한다) + 「のに」(역접).

questions.stem: この夏祭りは、100年以上前から、この町に住む人々によって（　　　）。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 続けられている is correct. It means "has been continued."

**Question Translation**  
This summer festival has been continued by the people living in this town for over 100 years.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes an ongoing action that the people of the town have been maintaining over a long period. The passive form 続けられている (つづけられている) is appropriate here because it indicates that the festival "has been continued" (passive form in progressive aspect), emphasizing the action taken by the people over time. This form is often used for phenomena or traditions that have been maintained by certain people or groups.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 続けてもらえる (つづけてもらえる) means "can have it continued." This implies asking someone a favor or permission to continue something, which does not fit the context where a tradition is simply described as being continued.
- Answer 2: 続けさせられる (つづけさせられる) means "be made to continue." This implies compulsion or being forced to continue, which is not the intended meaning when describing a traditional festival maintained by the community.
- Answer 4: 続けてくれている (つづけてくれている) means "continuing it for us." This suggests that it's being continued as a favor specifically to someone else, which doesn’t match the context of communal tradition being described as ongoing.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The passive form 続けられている is constructed from 続ける (to continue) and the passive auxiliary られる. The passive form often indicates that the subject (the festival) is the recipient of an action performed by an unspecified agent (the residents), making it ideal for conveying a sense of tradition or a customary practice sustained over time by a community. Recognizing how the passive voice is employed in this context is crucial for understanding nuances in Japanese sentence structures.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「続(つづ)けられている」 — 계속되고 있다(수동·진행).
오답: 1 続けてもらえる(부탁의 수혜), 2 続けさせられる(사역수동), 4 続けてくれている(나에게 해 주다)은 「町の人들에 의해」와 호응 안 됨.
포인트: 「Nによって + Vれている」 = N에 의해 ~되어 오고 있다.

questions.stem: A「このクッキー、おいしいね。どこで（　　　）？」B「さくらデパートだよ。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 買ったの is correct. It often serves as a casual way to ask for confirmation or information, equivalent to "where did you buy it?" in this context.

**Question Translation**  
A: "These cookies are delicious. Where did you buy them?"  
B: "At Sakura Department Store."

**Explanation**  
The phrase 「どこで買ったの？」 translates to "where did you buy them?" in English, where 買った (かった) is the past tense of 買う (かう), meaning "to buy," and の effectively turns the verb phrase into a question seeking clarification or information in a casual context. It directly asks B for the location where the cookies were purchased, fitting the natural flow of conversation between A and B. 

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 買ったよね means "you bought them, right?" It seeks agreement or confirmation on a shared understanding and doesn't inherently form a question specifically about the place of purchase.  
- Answer 3: 買ったことある means "have you ever bought them?" This is asking about past experience in general, not about the specific occurrence relevant here.  
- Answer 4: 買ったんじゃない means "didn't you buy them?" or "someone else bought them," which implies doubt or seeks confirmation rather than directly asking where the purchasing took place.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The particle の is often used at the end of a sentence to soften it or to turn a statement into a question when seeking information or clarification, especially in informal contexts. It's a crucial part of colloquial Japanese, especially when expressing curiosity or inquiry in a conversational manner.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「買(か)ったの」 — 산 거야?(친근한 질문).
오답: 2 ね(확인), 3 ことある(경험), 4 んじゃない(추측)는 「어디서 샀어?」 단순 질문과 안 어울림.
포인트: 친한 사이의 의문형 「Vたの？」.

questions.stem: （会議室で）A「会議、お疲れさまでした。この会議室、あとでまた使うので、エアコンはつけたままに（　　　）。」B「はい、わかりました。」
opts: なっておいてください
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: しておいてください is correct. It means "please leave (it) as it is" or "please leave it on."

**Question Translation**  
(In the meeting room)  
A: "Thank you for the meeting. We'll use this meeting room again later, so please leave the air conditioner on."  
B: "Yes, understood."

**Explanation**  
The sentence requires a form that indicates a request to keep a certain condition ("leave the air conditioner on") for future use. The phrase "しておいてください" is a common construction in Japanese that involves the verb "して" (the て-form of する) followed by "おく" to indicate that the action should be done in preparation for the future, and "ください" makes it a polite request. Therefore, "つけたままにしておいてください" suggests keeping the air conditioner running for later use, fitting the context perfectly.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: していませんか uses the negative question form, which would mean "isn't it turned on?" This doesn't fit the context where a request to keep the air conditioner on is needed.
- Answer 2: なっていませんか uses a similar negative question structure with the verb "なる" (to become), meaning "isn't it on?" Again, this does not fit the context of making a request.
- Answer 4: なっておいてください suggests "please be in a state," which is awkward and doesn't grammatically combine with "つけたまま" to make a coherent sentence regarding keeping an appliance running.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The construction "～ておく" is often used to indicate doing something in advance or maintaining a current state for future convenience, and when combined with "ください," it forms a polite request. Understanding this pattern is crucial for interpreting requests or instructions in Japanese, especially in contexts involving preparation or maintenance of a state.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「しておいてください」 — (그대로) 해 두세요.
오답: 1 していませんか(있지 않습니까), 2 なっていませんか(되어 있지 않나요), 4 なっておいてください(되어 있어 주세요)는 의지적 부탁 표현이 아님.
포인트: 「Vておく」 = 미리 ~해 두다. 「つけたままにしておく」 = 켠 채로 두다.

questions.stem: 昨日読んだ雑誌に、「忘れることは、必ずしも（　　　）」と書いてあった。
opts: 悪いことかもしれない
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 悪いことではない is correct. It means "it is not necessarily a bad thing."

**Question Translation**  
In the magazine I read yesterday, it was written that "forgetting is not necessarily a bad thing."

**Explanation**  
The phrase 必ずしも (かならずしも) indicates "not necessarily," and it is commonly used with ではない to express that something is not definitively true or that there is some room for exception. Hence, 必ずしも悪いことではない correctly translates to "is not necessarily a bad thing," which fits the context of discussing the idea posed in the magazine article.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 悪いことなのだ suggests a definite conclusion that "it is a bad thing." This contradicts the usage of 必ずしも, which expresses uncertainty or an exception.
- Answer 3: 悪いことのはずだ implies a strong expectation that "it ought to be a bad thing." This does not align with the nuance of 必ずしも, which does not support definitive statements.
- Answer 4: 悪いことかもしれない implies "it might be a bad thing," which reflects possibility but does not fit with the idiomatic pairing of 必ずしも with ではない.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
必ずしも is an adverb used to indicate that something isn't always or necessarily the case. It is typically used to introduce sentences that clarify exceptions or offer alternative perspectives. The structure 必ずしも～ではない is a common grammatical pattern in Japanese, used to denote that something is not invariably true. Recognizing this pattern is essential for understanding nuances in statements that make exceptions or contrast general assumptions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「悪(わる)いことではない」 — 나쁜 일은 아니다.
오답: 「必(かなら)ずしも〜ない」(반드시 ~인 것은 아니다)와 호응. 1 ~이다, 3 ~일 것이다, 4 ~일지 모른다는 부분 부정과 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「必ずしも〜ではない」 = 반드시 ~인 것은 아니다.

questions.stem: 学生「先生は、ふだん何かスポーツを（　　　）。」先生「ええ。ゴルフにはときどき行きますよ。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: なさいますか is correct. It means "do you do (something)?" using the honorific form, which is appropriate when a student is addressing a teacher.

**Question Translation**  
Student: "Sensei, what do you usually do for sports?"   
Teacher: "Yes, I occasionally go golfing."

**Explanation**  
The sentence requires an honorific expression as it concerns a student speaking to a teacher, maintaining respect. なさいますか is the honorific form of "する" (to do), making it the best choice to politely inquire about the teacher's sports activities.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: いたしますか is the humble form, not used when asking politely about something others do, especially superiors like a teacher. 
- Answer 2: まいりますか is also a humble form, which means "to go" or "to come" and is inappropriate for this question's context.
- Answer 4: いらっしゃいますか is the honorific form of "to be/go/come," which doesn't fit grammatically as the sentence is about activities (doing sports).

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Using the correct level of politeness and honorifics is crucial in Japanese, especially in contexts involving social hierarchy such as teacher-student relationships. なさいますか is the appropriate way to ask about someone’s actions respectfully.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「なさいますか」 — 하십니까(존경어).
오답: 1 いたします(겸양), 2 まいります(겸양, 行く/来る), 4 いらっしゃいます(존경, 行く/来る/いる)는 「する」의 존경어가 아님.
포인트: 「する」의 존경어 = 「なさる」.

questions.stem: 朝、近所のパン屋の前を通ると、パン____ ____ ★ ____する。
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)
Answer 4: いいにおい

Correct Order
3 → 2 → 4 → 1
📌 の → 焼ける → いいにおい★ → が

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown
③ の — genitive; makes “パンの …”.
② 焼ける — “(is) baking”.
④ いいにおい — “nice smell / good aroma”.
① が — subject marker for におい.

Reconstructed Sentence
朝、近所のパン屋の前を通ると、**パンの焼けるいいにおいがする**。
→ “When I pass by the neighborhood bakery in the morning, there’s the nice smell of bread baking.”

Why This Order Works
“X の 焼ける におい” = “the smell of X baking”. **いいにおい** takes **がする** to express “smells nice”.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=세 번째 「いいにおい」). 올바른 순서: の→焼ける→いいにおい→が.
포인트: 「パンの焼けるいいにおいがする」 = 빵 굽는 좋은 냄새가 난다. 「Nのにおいがする」.

questions.stem: 平日はなかなか運動する時間がないので、日常生活の中で、エレベーターではなくて階段を____ ____ ____ ★ とかしている。
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)
Answer 2: 歩く

Correct Order
3 → 1 → 2 → 4
📌 使う → とか → ★歩く → スピードを速くする

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown
③ 使う — “(I) use (the stairs)”; first example action.
① とか — listing marker “like/for example”.
★② 歩く — modifies the following noun to form 「歩くスピード」 (“walking speed”).
④ スピードを速くする — “make (my) speed faster; walk faster”; second example action.

Reconstructed Sentence
平日はなかなか運動する時間がないので、日常生活の中で、**エレベーターではなくて階段を使うとか、歩くスピードを速くする**とかしている。
→ “I don’t have much time to exercise on weekdays, so in daily life I do things like use the stairs instead of the elevator or walk faster.”

Why This Order Works
The pattern **〜とか〜とかしている** lists habitual examples. **とか** must follow the first action (**使う**) to mark it as one example. **歩く** must come immediately before **スピード** to create the natural noun phrase **歩くスピード**, and **速くする** states the action of increasing that speed.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=네 번째 「スピードを速くする」). 올바른 순서: 使う→とか→歩く→スピードを速くする.
포인트: 「Aとか、B(とか)」 = A라든가 B라든가. 「階段を使う」 + 「歩くスピードを速くする」.

questions.stem:
森田「西山さん、新しいアルバイトはどうですか。」
西山「始めた____ ____ ★ ____大変ですが、楽しいです。」
opts: 覚えなければいけない
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)
Answer 3: ことが

Correct Order
4 → 2 → 3 → 1
📌 ばかりなので → 覚えなければいけない → ことが★ → 多くて

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown
④ ばかりなので — “since I just started”.
② 覚えなければいけない — “must learn/remember”.
③ ことが — nominalises the clause: “there are things that …”.
① 多くて — “(they are) many, so …”.

Reconstructed Sentence
「**始めたばかりなので、覚えなければいけないことが多くて**大変ですが、楽しいです。」
→ “Since I just started, there are many things I have to learn, so it’s tough, but it’s fun.”

Why This Order Works
The reason **始めたばかりなので** comes first. **覚えなければいけないことが多い** is a fixed, natural pattern for “there are many things I must learn.”
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 (★자리=세 번째 「ことが」). 올바른 순서: ばかりなので→覚えなければいけない→ことが→多くて.
포인트: 「Vたばかり」(막 ~함) + 「Vなければいけないことが多い」(해야 할 것이 많다).

questions.stem: 初めて登山をしたとき、山の上からの景色を見て、「____ ____ ★ ____」と感動した。
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)
Answer 4: 眺め

Correct Order
2 → 1 → 4 → 3
📌 なんて → きれいな → 眺め★ → なんだろう

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown
② なんて — exclamatory “what …”.
① きれいな — adjective modifying the noun.
④ 眺め — “view / scenery”.
③ なんだろう — “it must be / how …”, exclamatory close.

Reconstructed Sentence
「**なんてきれいな眺めなんだろう**」と感動した。
→ “I was moved and thought, ‘What a beautiful view!’”

Why This Order Works
The set phrase **なんて + (adj) + N + なんだろう** expresses admiration/exclamation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=세 번째 「眺(なが)め」). 올바른 순서: なんて→きれいな→眺め→なんだろう.
포인트: 「なんて〜なんだろう」(얼마나 ~인가) 감탄 구문. 「なんてきれいな眺めなんだろう」.

questions.stem:
（会社で）
田中「ねえ、知ってる？営業課の山下さん、来月「____ ____ ★ ____。」
石川「え、そうなんだ。知らなかった。」
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)
Answer 1: 結婚するんだ

Correct Order
4 → 3 → 1 → 2
📌 受付の林さん → と → 結婚するんだ★ → って

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown
④ 受付の林さん — “Ms. Hayashi at reception”.
③ と — “with / (quote) that”.
① 結婚するんだ — “(they’re) getting married”.
② って — casual hearsay marker “I heard / they say”.

Reconstructed Sentence
田中「ねえ、知ってる？営業課の山下さん、来月**受付の林さんと結婚するんだって**。」
→ “Hey, did you hear? Yamashita from Sales is getting married to Ms. Hayashi at reception next month.”

Why This Order Works
**A と結婚する** is the fixed pattern for marrying someone; **～んだって** reports hearsay casually.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 (★자리=세 번째 「結婚(けっこん)するんだ」). 올바른 순서: 受付の林さん→と→結婚するんだ→って.
포인트: 「Nと結婚する」 + 「〜って」(전문, ~래).
